name: Bracarense Portuguese

allophone_rules.notes: Northern betacism (Cintra's all-Northern trait 1), realised strongly in Braga: the /v/ ~ /b/ opposition is lost and both merge into a single phoneme /b/, surfacing as the stop [b] or the softer spirant [β] (vou -> [bow], vaca -> [ˈbakɐ], vacina -> [bɐˈsinɐ]). Cintra (1971:87): 'o desaparecimento da oposição fonológica entre os fonemas /v/ e /b/ e a sua fusão num fonema único /b/, realizado ora como oclusiva, ora como fricativa (ou espirante) b ou β' — the [β] variant is recorded in the allophones inventory. NATIVE ATTESTATION: the Braga speaker Miguel in Portuguese With Leo, 'Sotaque e expressões de Braga' (native-speaker source, https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xaen3F8256M): 'pões o B em vez do V ... muitos sons que em português standard seriam Vvvv, mas vocês dizem um B ... às vezes é um B mais pronunciado, outras vezes é um B mais suave tipo Bou' (vou -> bou); and 'no norte diz-se bácina, às vezes assim com um ligeiro B' (vacina). Modelled as a post-lexical phoneme-side rewrite so the merger applies uniformly however /v/ arises from the inherited grapheme map.

Diphthongisation of the tonic CLOSE-mid front vowel [e] -> rising [je] (mês -> [ˈmjeʃ], ele -> [ˈjelɨ]), the diagnostic diphthongisation of the Baixo-Minho / Douro-Litoral zone (of which Braga, in the Minho, is part). Cintra (1971): 'a ditongação, tão caracterizadora, das vogais tónicas fechadas [e] em [je], [o] em [wo]'; Segura (2013) names diphthongisation of stressed vowels the characteristic trait of the north-western variety. NATIVE ATTESTATION: the 8-accents overview (Portuguese With Leo, https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pitj0XxYO7I) records the Braga 'o quê' -> 'o quiê' (insertion of the [j] onglide: quê [ke] -> [kje]) and 'mieu' (meu). Gated to the CLOSE [e] only (genuinely open tonic [ɛ] does NOT diphthongise); same pre-lexical open/close LIMIT as pt-PT-x-porto — words whose standard value is close [e] but which the base engine transcribes [ɛ] (cedo, medo) escape the rule, which is deliberately not widened to [ɛ]. Brissos (2018; Brissos & Rodrigues 2016) shows the OPEN mids diphthongize too — see the companion *_DIPHTHONGISE_E_OPEN/_O_OPEN rules; the earlier 'genuinely open [ɛ]/[ɔ] does NOT diphthongise' caution is superseded by that stronger acoustic source.

Diphthongisation of the tonic CLOSE-mid back vowel [o] -> rising [wo] (avô -> [ɐˈbwo], pôr -> [ˈpwoɾ]), the back-vowel counterpart of BRA_DIPHTHONGISE_E in the same Cintra (1971) Baixo-Minho passage; Segura (2013). Gated to CLOSE [o]; genuinely open tonic [ɔ] does not diphthongise. Same pre-lexical open/close LIMIT as pt-PT-x-porto. Brissos (2018; Brissos & Rodrigues 2016) shows the OPEN mids diphthongize too — see the companion *_DIPHTHONGISE_E_OPEN/_O_OPEN rules; the earlier 'genuinely open [ɛ]/[ɔ] does NOT diphthongise' caution is superseded by that stronger acoustic source.

Diphthongisation of the tonic OPEN-mid front vowel: stressed open [ɛ] -> rising [jɛ] (pé -> [ˈpjɛ], café -> [kɐˈfjɛ]). Brissos (2018, pp.196-197, 200-201; reporting Brissos & Rodrigues 2016, AVOC + ALEPG/ALE) overturns the earlier close-mid-only view: NW diphthongization applies to the open mids too ('[ˈpi̯ɛ] pé'), so that only the cardinal vowels stay phonologically stable. This also RESOLVES the open/close pre-lexical mis-selection that the close-only DIPHTHONGISE_E documented as a limit — whichever mid the base engine selects for a spelling-unmarked stressed <e>, it now diphthongizes (to [je] or [jɛ]). Restricted to the stressed nucleus. NOTES-ONLY (not modelled): the co-existing toward-central subtype RD2 ([ɛɐ̯]) and the marginal /i/->[ei̯].

Diphthongisation of the tonic OPEN-mid back vowel: stressed open [ɔ] -> rising [wɔ] (toque -> [ˈtwɔkɨ], só -> [ˈswɔ]; and the emblematic Porto, whose base-engine open [ɔ] now diphthongizes to [ˈpwɔɾtu], approaching the attested close [ˈpu̯oɾtu]). Brissos (2018, pp.196-197, 200-201; Brissos & Rodrigues 2016) — open mids diphthongize in the NW. Restricted to the stressed nucleus. NOTES-ONLY (not modelled): the co-existing toward-central subtype RD2 ([ɔɐ̯]) and the marginal /i/->[ei̯].

notes: Bracarense (Braga) European Portuguese — a Northern (Minho) variety of the Cintra Baixo-Minho / Douro-Litoral zone, described natively as HEAVIER / more strongly Northern than Porto (Portuguese With Leo, 'Sotaque e expressões de Braga', native-speaker source, https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xaen3F8256M, and the 8-accents overview https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pitj0XxYO7I). A DELTA spec over the broad pt-PT base; it inherits the whole standard EP system (unstressed vowel reduction, dark coda /l/, coda-sibilant chiado, sandhi) and declares only the Northern deltas. MODELLED FEATURES: (1) BETACISM — /v/~/b/ merge in /b/ (~[β]) (allophone rule BRA_BETACISM), attested more categorically than Porto's variable merger: Miguel 'em Braga vai-me sair naturalmente trocar o V pelo B'. (2) TONIC-CLOSE-VOWEL DIPHTHONGISATION — [e]->[je], [o]->[wo] (BRA_DIPHTHONGISE_E/_O), the Cintra Baixo-Minho marker, natively attested via 'quiê' (quê) and 'mieu' (meu). (3) DIPHTHONG PRESERVATION — <ou>->[ow] (Miguel: 'tu dizes OU, dizes vOU ou bOU') and <ei>->[ej] kept where Lisbon monophthongises/lowers ([o], [ɐj]) (inherited-map grapheme deltas). NOT MODELLED (documented, not faked): (a) the northern 'vogais mais abertas' / prosodic lengthening for emphasis (Miguel: 'abres mais o A em cantÁdo ... prolongar algumas sílabas quando queremos dar uma ênfase especial') and the 'mais musical / mais cantado' cadence are gradient prosodic traits, not segmental rules. (b) The word-final paragogic /r/ release in -ar verbs (Miguel: 'vocês dizem tipo Faláre, gostarE ... a meio caminho entre o ehrr mais inglês e o ere mais certinho') is variable ('já não foi tanto') and hard to gate to infinitives orthographically; left inherited. (c) The 'beim' (bem) realisation is nasal-diphthong detail left to a downstream engine. (d) 'Vós ides / vinde / ide' is a morphosyntactic, not phonological, feature. LIMIT (shared with pt-PT-x-porto): the diphthongisation rules are gated to CLOSE mid vowels; because open/close for spelling-unmarked stressed <e>/<o> is lexical and the inherited pt-PT map defaults to the OPEN allophone, some underlying-close words (cedo, medo) escape diphthongisation — widening to the open vowels would wrongly diphthongise genuinely open tonic vowels (café, avó) and is rejected. The apico-alveolar coda sibilant (Cintra's primary North/South isogloss) is an articulatory distinction not simulable at the phoneme/orthography level. ACOUSTIC UPDATE (Brissos 2018; Brissos & Rodrigues 2016): the OPEN mids diphthongize too ([ɛ]->[jɛ], [ɔ]->[wɔ]); modelled as BRA_DIPHTHONGISE_E_OPEN/_O_OPEN. RD2 (toward-central) and the marginal /i/->[ei̯] left to notes.

ancestors.notes: Standard (Lisbon-type) European Portuguese; Braga diverges by (strong) Northern betacism (v->b/β), tonic-close-vowel diphthongisation ([e]->[je], [o]->[wo]), and diphthong preservation (<ou>->[ow], <ei>->[ej]).

sources.author: Portuguese With Leo (with Miguel, bracarense)
sources.title: Sotaque e expressões de Braga
sources.notes: Native-speaker EP accent deep-dive (NOT academic). Attests, with example words: Northern betacism v->b/[β] ('pões o B em vez do V', 'vou/bou', 'vacina -> bácina, às vezes com um ligeiro B'); diphthong preservation <ou>->[ow] ('tu dizes OU, dizes vOU ou bOU'); more open vowels and prosodic lengthening ('abres mais o A em cantÁdo', 'mais musical, mais cantado'); and the variable word-final paragogic -r in -ar verbs ('Faláre', 'gostarE'). Braga framed as a distinct, heavier Northern accent.

sources.author: Portuguese With Leo
sources.title: The 8 Portuguese accents of Portugal
sources.notes: Native-speaker EP accent breakdown (NOT academic). Records the Braga tonic-close-vowel diphthongisation via 'o quê' -> 'o quiê' (quê [ke] -> [kje]) and 'mieu' (meu), and 'beim' (bem); grounds the BRA_DIPHTHONGISE_E reading together with Cintra's Baixo-Minho diphthongisation isogloss.

sources.author: Cintra, Luís F. Lindley
sources.title: Nova proposta de classificação dos dialectos galego-portugueses
sources.publisher: Boletim de Filologia (Centro de Estudos Filológicos, Lisboa), vol. 22, pp. 81-116
sources.notes: Foundational classification of the Galician-Portuguese dialect space. Trait 1 (betacism, p.87): 'o desaparecimento da oposição fonológica entre os fonemas /v/ e /b/ e a sua fusão num fonema único /b/, realizado ora como oclusiva, ora como fricativa (ou espirante) b ou β'. The Baixo-Minho / Douro-Litoral (of which Braga, in the Minho, is part) is delimited by 'a ditongação, tão caracterizadora, das vogais tónicas fechadas [e] em [je], [o] em [wo] (por vezes [wɔ])'. Grounds the modelled betacism and tonic-vowel diphthongisation.

sources.author: Segura, Luísa
sources.title: Variedades dialetais do português europeu
sources.publisher: In Raposo, E.B.P. et al. (eds.), Gramática do Português, vol. I, Fundação Calouste Gulbenkian, Lisboa, pp. 85-142
sources.notes: Modern EP dialectology synthesis. Names diphthongisation of stressed vowels as the characteristic trait of the north-western (Baixo-Minho / Douro-Litoral) variety and documents Northern betacism — the corroborating academic source for the Braga features modelled here.

sources.author: Mateus, M.H.M. & d'Andrade, E.
sources.title: The Phonology of Portuguese
sources.notes: Reference EP phonology for the inherited base processes (unstressed vowel reduction, dark coda /l/, coda sibilants) that Braga inherits unchanged from pt-PT.

sources.author: Brissos, Fernando
sources.title: Proposta de reformulação da caracterização dialetal do noroeste português
sources.publisher: Estudos de Lingüística Galega, Volume especial I: 193-208
sources.notes: Reformulates the diphthongizing 'variedade do noroeste' (Cintra's Baixo-Minho / Douro-Litoral), reporting AVOC + ALEPG/ALE data largely from Brissos & Rodrigues (2016). Central finding: tonic-vowel diphthongization is NOT limited to the CLOSE mids [e o] — it applies to the OPEN mids [ɛ ɔ] too ('[ˈpi̯ɛ] pé', '[ˈtu̯ɔkɨ] toque', pp.196-197, 200-201), so only the cardinal vowels stay phonologically stable; it also affects, marginally and contextually, the high front /i/ → [ei̯] ('[mɐˈkei̯ɐ] maquia', ~19% of tokens, pre-pause/vowel only, p.199). Two co-existing diphthong subtypes: within-axis (RD1: [i̯e i̯ɛ], [u̯o u̯ɔ]) and toward-central (RD2: [eɐ̯ ɛɐ̯], [oɐ̯ ɔɐ̯], pp.200-201). Grounds widening the diphthongization rules to the open mids; RD2 and the /i/-diphthong are documented but left unmodelled (co-existing variant / contextual).

sources.author: Brissos, Fernando & Celeste Rodrigues
sources.title: Vocalismo acentuado do Noroeste português — descrição acústica, variação dialectal e representação fonológica
sources.notes: The primary acoustic study behind Brissos (2018): AVOC formant data plus the dense ALEPG/ALE NW network. Argues the NW vowel systems require revising the close-mid-only diphthongization picture and that seven distinct vowel systems are needed to represent the continental dialects. Not open access; its examples and page-cited quadros are used here via the open-access Brissos (2018), which reproduces them.

stress.notes: Acordo Ortográfico 1990 conventions: paroxytone default; written accents override; final -i/-u/-r/-l/-z/-x/-im/-om/-um/-ns and nasal diphthongs attract final stress. Unmarked -em/-am stay paroxytone (homem, falam). DIPHTHONGS: declared so the bundled syllabifier counts nuclei correctly — without them every vowel run merges into one nucleus and co-e-lho is read as coe-lho, one syllable short, putting the stress and its vowel reduction on the wrong vowel. ⟨oe⟩ is hiatus, not a diphthong (Cunha & Cintra, §Ditongos).

location.notes: Braga — the city the Bracarense variety is named for. [Wikidata Q83247 P625]
